title: How Not to Be Wrong
subtitle: The Power of Mathematical Thinking
author: Jordan Ellenberg
themes: Mathematics as a tool for everyday reasoning
Survivorship bias and sample selection errors
The role of rigor and proof in thinking
How to ask the right question
awards: New York Times Notable Book of 2014
Euler Book Prize (Mathematical Association of America, 2015)
Morning Calm Book of the Year (Korean)
excerpt: The math we learn in school is not math — it is a compressed version of math that hides the thinking behind the answers. Jordan Ellenberg invites us to see mathematics as the science of not being wrong: a framework for asking better questions, testing assumptions, and navigating uncertainty in everyday life. This book is about how mathematical thinking can make us more rigorous, less gullible, and better at separating noise from signal.
